coredata.dc:title: Biofilm inhibition and bacterial eradication by C-dots derived from polyethyleneimine-citric acid
coredata.dc:creator: Abraham, Wakeem L.
Demirci, Sahin
Wypyski, Madison S.
Ayyala, Ramesh S.
Bhethanabotla, Venkat R.
Lawson, Louise B.
Sahiner, Nurettin
coredata.dc:description: Protonable polyethyleneimine (PEI) and citric acid (CA) based C-dots were prepared via a hydrothermal process, yielding particles with a hydrodynamic diameter of ~100 and ~80 nm, and zeta potential of − 2.3 ± 0.1 and + 23.4 ± 0.2 mV for PEI-CA C-dots and their protonated form, respectively. Treating Staphylococcus aureus with these C-dots resulted in a statistically significant reduction in bacterial growth, specifically growth in the planktonic phase as well as in the development of bacterial biofilm when compared to untreated (p < 0.05). When 24 h matured S. aureus biofilms were treated with C-dots, a significant disruption and dispersion of bacteria from the existing biofilms was noted as compared to untreated (p < 0.05). Other Gram-positive microorganisms (B. cereus, S. epidermidis, S. pyogenes) and Gram-negative (P. mirabilis, E. coli, and P. aeruginosa) were also susceptible to the antimicrobial activity of the PEI-CA C-dots with significant inhibition of bacterial growth noted for all organisms after C-dot treatment. Only for B. cereus, S. epidermidis, and P. aeruginosa was this reduction in total growth reflected in decreased planktonic growth. However, biofilm formation by all organisms was reduced significantly upon treatment with the C-dots, including those for which planktonic growth was not impacted.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Antibiofilm
Bacterial eradication
Biofilm inhibition
Carbon dot
Antibacterial surface

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of N-doped C-dots
Inhibition and Eradication of S. aureus Biofilms
N-doped C-dot-HCl biofilm inhibition with other relevant organisms

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: University of South Florida Morsani College of Medicine

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the ASPiRE Program at Tulane University School of Medicine and the Tulane University Honors Summer Research Program . This work was also partially funded by the University of South Florida start up fund to NS.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic presentation of synthesis of N-doped C-dots and protonation step, and SEM images of (b) N-doped C-dots and (c) N-doped C-dot-HCl.

SEM-EDX atomic mapping of N-doped C-dots for (a) O, (b) N, and (c) Cl atoms, and of N-doped C-dot-HCl for (d) O, (e) N, and (f) Cl atoms (scale bar: 3 µm), and (g) EDX patterns of N-doped C-dots (dashed line) and N-doped C-dot-HCl (solid line). ﻿Inset shows the atomic percentage of N, O and Cl in the samples.
(a) FT-IR spectra and (b) TGA thermograms of N-doped C-dot and N-doped C-dot-HCl.
(a) pH vs zeta potential graphs, and (b) XRD patterns of prepared N-doped C-dots and N-doped C-dot-HCl.
Fluorescence-emission spectra of N-doped C-dots and N-doped C-dot-HCl.

Antibiofilm properties of N-doped C-dots against S. aureus. (a) Biofilm inhibition by N-doped C-dot-HCl was assessed at increasing particle concentrations by treating bacterial cultures at t = 0 and incubating for 24 h. (b) To assess eradication of mature biofilms, previously untreated biofilms were treated with C-dots at t = 24 h and quantified at t = 48 h. (c) For comparison, biofilm inhibition by gentamicin was assessed at t = 0. (d) Existing biofilms were treated with gentamicin 24-hours post-inoculation to assess eradication. Absorbance was read at 600 nm for total and planktonic growth and at 575 nm for biofilms. All bars represent mean + SEM (n ≥ 4). *Indicates statistical significance as compared to untreated cultures (p < 0.05).

Antibiofilm properties of N-doped C-dots against medically relevant Gram positive and Gram-negative bacteria. Indicated organisms were treated with N-doped C-dots at the time of inoculation. Cultures were incubated for 24 h, at which point total, planktonic and biofilm growth was quantified. Absorbance was read at 600 nm for total and planktonic growth and 575 nm for biofilms. Bars represent mean + SEM (n ≥ 3). *Indicates statistical significance as compared to untreated cultures (p < 0.05).
The effect of protonation on surface charge, size, and quantum yield of N-doped C-dots.

head.ce:title: Biofilm inhibition and bacterial eradication by C-dots derived from polyethyleneimine-citric acid

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biomedical Engineering, and Materials Science and Engineering Program, University of South Florida

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Biomedical Engineering, and Materials Science and Engineering Program, University of South Florida, Tampa, FL 33620, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Protonable polyethyleneimine (PEI) and citric acid (CA) based C-dots were prepared via a hydrothermal process, yielding particles with a hydrodynamic diameter of ~100 and ~80 nm, and zeta potential of − 2.3 ± 0.1 and + 23.4 ± 0.2 mV for PEI-CA C-dots and their protonated form, respectively. Treating Staphylococcus aureus with these C-dots resulted in a statistically significant reduction in bacterial growth, specifically growth in the planktonic phase as well as in the development of bacterial biofilm when compared to untreated (p < 0.05). When 24 h matured S. aureus biofilms were treated with C-dots, a significant disruption and dispersion of bacteria from the existing biofilms was noted as compared to untreated (p < 0.05). Other Gram-positive microorganisms (B. cereus, S. epidermidis, S. pyogenes) and Gram-negative (P. mirabilis, E. coli, and P. aeruginosa) were also susceptible to the antimicrobial activity of the PEI-CA C-dots with significant inhibition of bacterial growth noted for all organisms after C-dot treatment. Only for B. cereus, S. epidermidis, and P. aeruginosa was this reduction in total growth reflected in decreased planktonic growth. However, biofilm formation by all organisms was reduced significantly upon treatment with the C-dots, including those for which planktonic growth was not impacted.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Biofilms are complex three-dimensional communities of microbial cells encased within an insoluble matrix of primarily polysaccharides. These microcolonies are typically polymicrobial and may contain a combination of bacterial, fungal and/or protist species. Biofilms naturally form as microorganisms accumulate at a solid-liquid interface and irreversibly attach to the solid surface. Surface attachment and increasing cell density at the surface trigger specific alterations in gene expression [1]. As more and more cells adhere, quorum sensing molecules secreted by the microorganisms facilitate cell-cell signaling, allowing each organism to sense the presence and density of others nearby [2]. Metabolic changes result, including formation of the extracellular matrix that encases the biofilm [3].

Microorganisms that are commonly associated with biofilms are Gram-positive Staphylococcus epidermis, Staphylococcus aureus and Enterococcus species; Gram-negative Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Escherichia coli, Enterobacter species and Klebsiella; and lastly Candida spp [4]. For example, Klebsiella pneumoniae and Enterococcus faecalis cause biofilm-related urinary tract infections; Pseudomonas aeruginosa biofilms within the lung mucosa can lead to chronic infections, particularly in patients with cystic fibrosis; and non-typeable Haemophilus influenzae and Streptococcus pneumoniae cause otitis media [5]. Other than diseases that result directly from biofilm formation in/on host tissue, device-related biofilm infections can also occur on surgical implants, within catheters, and even sutures. Another device that has potential for biofilm formation is contact lenses. Microorganisms that commonly adhere to contact lenses include E. coli, P. aeruginosa, S. aureus, S. epidermidis, Candida, Serratia and Proteus species [4]. In particular, P. aeruginosa is a common cause of keratitis, leading to inflammation of the cornea [6,7]. Because of their complex structure, biofilms present increased challenges in treating clinical infections.

In addition to the impact on human health, biofilms can cause problems in industrial settings as well. Microfouling caused by biofilms and the resulting macrofouling by larger aquatic organisms on ship hulls increases frictional resistance and, therefore, fuel charges for maritime ships [8]. Biofilm formation within commercial, residential and automobile air conditioning systems can lead to poor air quality [9], and even infections caused by aerosol inhalation. Many water sources such as industrial cooling towers, hospital and/or public hot water systems, swimming pools and spas, and public fountains provide moist environments for pathogens to colonize and potentially cause infections, such as Legionella related diseases.

In water distribution systems, biofilms pose a huge challenge in purification and delivery processes and play a role in pipe corrosion as well as affecting the taste, smell, and color of the water [10]. Although biofilms have variations in their thickness, both spatially and over time, their overall thickness can be up to a few hundred micrometers. The multilayered structure and the extracellular matrix inhibit diffusion of antimicrobials and disinfecting agents into and throughout the biofilms, limiting accessibility to microorganisms deeper within the biofilm. In addition, reduced nutrient availability within the biofilm structure lowers metabolic activity that also reduces susceptibility to many antimicrobials and disinfectants. Microorganisms within biofilms are more resistant to antibiotics and disinfecting agents by as much as up to 100–1000-fold as compared to bacteria growing in the planktonic phase [5,6]. Development of novel antimicrobial compounds that target biofilms in particular would provide solutions to the clinical and industrial consequences of biofilm formation and related diseases.

Carbon-based nanodots (C-dots), which are spherical particles consisting of carbon, hydrogen and oxygen atoms, and sometimes other heteroatom doping e.g., nitrogen (N), boron (B), phosphorous (P), have gained great interest recently in the fields of bio-imaging, drug delivery, diagnostics, drug delivery systems, sensors, medical diagnostics, bio-catalysis, bio-electronics, photo-catalysis and electro-catalysis [11–21]. We recently demonstrated the synthesis of nitrogen-doped C-dots using a hydrothermal synthesis technique from polyethyleneimine (PEI) and citric acid (CA) [22]. Altering PEI:CA weight ratios allowed us to tailor the hydrodynamic diameter from ~10 to ~70 nm and zeta potential from 1.3 to − 1.57 mV. These particles inhibited bacterial replication with a higher sensitivity noted by Gram-positive organisms than Gram-negative; however, the particles showed no toxicity to mammalian cells [22]. Wang et al., demonstrated the antibacterial and antibiofilm properties of ammonium-doped C-dots against S. aureus [23] . Similarly, Otis et al., demonstrated antibacterial and antibiofilm properties for aminoguanidine C-dots towards P. aeruginosa. The selectivity was demonstrated by their ineffectiveness towards Gram-positive S. aureus and B. cereus [17]. This demonstrates the possibility for tailoring C-dot formulations to fit more specific antibacterial and antibiofilm applications. The surface chemical properties of nanoparticles have been implicated in their ability to penetrate biofilms. Tailoring surface charge and size by the addition of functional groups can enhance the ability of nanoparticles to infiltrate the biofilm extracellular polymeric barrier, with cationic charge and smaller sizes being favorable [22].

It was reported by Zhang et al., that the bacteria quorum detection system, which is critical in biofilm formation, can be induced by the water-soluble cationic polymers and extend the time period of quorum signal molecules creation [25]. Therefore, the biofilm formation time can be extended and even the biofilm can be destroyed with cationic polymers [25,26]. It also was reported that cationic groups have a greater effect on the destruction of biofilms than hydrophobicity, and the reason for the physical degradation of the biofilm matrix was suggested for the binding of the cationic groups to negatively charged biopolymers in the biofilm [27]. Because of the demonstrated antimicrobial activity and the suitable physicochemical properties of the polyethyleneimine-derived C-dots, we hypothesized that PEI:CA C-dots would exhibit significant antibiofilm activity after protonation process. Our objective was to demonstrate the ability of these particles to inhibit biofilm production and eradicate biofilms generated by medically relevant Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacterial pathogens.

Citric acid monohydrate (CA, Carlo Erba, >99%) was used as a carbon source and polyethyleneimine (PEI, %50 in water, Mn:1800, Sigma Aldrich) as an N-doping agent for the synthesis of N-doped C-dots. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, Fisher BioReagents) and Mueller Hinton Broth (Criterion) were prepared according to manufacturer’s instructions. Double distilled water was used for the synthesis and characterization of N-doped C-dots and deionized water was used in the biofilm inhibition and eradication studies.

The N-doped C-dots were synthesized by a hydrothermal method in a Teflon-lined autoclave in accordance with a reported procedure [22]. Briefly, 1.0 g of CA was dissolved into a Teflon-lined autoclave in 22 mL of water. Then, 6.0 mL of an aqueous PEI solution (50 wt%) was added into the CA solution and stirred for 5 min at room temperature. Next, the autoclave was placed into the furnace and heated to 250 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min. The autoclave was kept in a furnace at 250 °C for 4 h. The obtained C-dot formulation was placed into 500 mL water within a dialysis membrane (molecular weight cut-off ≥12,000 Da) to wash the prepared N-doped C-dots (PEI-CA 3:1 w/w) for 3 h by changing the washing water every 30 min. After that, the washed N-doped C-dots were collected in a 250 mL round bottom flask and evaporated to concentrate the particles and about 80% of water was evaporated at room temperature using a rotary evaporator.

The prepared N-doped C-dots were treated with 10 mL of 5 M HCl for 10 min to promote protonation of the amine groups. The N-doped C-dot-HCl was added dropwise into an excess amount of acetone to precipitate the particles, which were then dried in an oven at 50 °C. The prepared N-doped C-dot and N-doped C-dot-HCl were stored in air-tight tubes for further use.

The morphologies of N-doped C-dots before and after HCl treatment were investigated by scanning electron microscope (FEI Quanta 200F) operated at an accelerating voltage of 20 kV. N-doped C-dots were dispersed in DI water, casted on a silicon wafer and allowed to dry at ambient temperature. The samples were sputtered with a gold palladium (Au–Pd) layer (~few nm) using Precision Etching Coating System (PECS) (Gatan, Model 682) before SEM analysis. The elemental compositions of the samples were also analyzed by using SEM equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometry (SDD-EDX detector EDAX Apollo X) operated at an accelerating voltage of 15 kV. Uncoated samples were monitored by SEM-EDX for ﻿the presence of N, O and Cl.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR, Perkin Elmer) spectra of prepared N-doped C-dots from 3:1 w/w ratios of PEI and CA were recorded between 4000 and 650 cm−1 range with 4 cm−1 resolution using ATR technique with diamond crystal as average of 4 scan.

The thermal stability of the prepared N-doped C-dot and N-doped C-dot-HCl were compared from thermograms obtained from Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, SII TG/DTA 6300, Exstar) thermograms in two steps. In the first step, the moisture was removed from the sample weighing ~3–5 mg by heating it to 100 °C with 200 mL/min flow rate N2 gas and 10 °C/min heating rate. Then, the graph of weight loss percent versus temperature up to 800 °C from 90 °C was recorded with 10 °C/min heating rate in the presence of 200 mL/min flow rate of N2 gas.

Zeta potential measurements of the prepared N-doped C-dots were done by using a zeta potential analyzer (Zetasizer Nano ZS, Malvern) using 1.0 mg/mL of N-doped C-dots in water. The change on surface charges of N-doped C-dot and N-doped C-dot-HCl at various pH values were measured in 1.0 mM of NaCl solution, and the pH of the solutions were adjusted by using 0.5 M of HCl and NaOH solutions.

Dynamic light scattering (DLS, Brookhaven Ins. Corp., 90 Plus/BIMAS) measurements were also carried out for the determination of size of prepared N-doped C-dot and N-doped C-dot-HCl particles from their corresponding solutions at 1.0 mg/mL concentration in double distilled water.

X-ray powder diffraction patterns of the N-doped C-dot and N-doped C-dot-HCl were recorded by a PANalytical X′Pert Pro MPD diffractometer equipped with CuKα radiation and the X′Celerator detector on diffracted beam. The XRD data were collected in a Bragg Brentano (θ/θ) vertical geometry operating in flat reflection mode between 3° and 70° (2θ) in steps of 0.02° 2θ with 1 s step-counting time. The X-ray tube operating at 45 kV and 40 mA was used and 1/2° divergence slit, a 0.04 rad soller slit and a 10 mm fixed mask was placed in the incident beam pathway. The High Score Plus (v.4.6.0) software was used for peak identification and automated search-match to analyze diffraction patterns.

Optical properties of N-doped C-dot and N-doped C-dot-HCl were investigated using UV–Vis spectroscopy (T80 + UV/VIS spectrometer, PG Instrument Ltd), and Fluorescence Spectroscopy (Lumina, Thermo) at room temperature.

Quantum yield percent (QY%) values for N-doped C-dots and N-doped C-dot-HCl were calculated using Eq. 1 via Quinine sulfate solution as a standard. Quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.5 M H2SO4 was used as a standard with a known QY% value of 54 % at 345 nm excitation wavelength for quinine sulfate.
QY% = QYStd% (I/IStd) (ODStd/OD) (ƞ2/ƞ2 Std)
Where “QY%” is fluorescence quantum yield, “I” is the integrated fluorescence intensity, “OD” is the UV–Vis absorbance, and “ƞ” is the refractive index of the solvent for C-dots suspension solution as water (ƞ = 1.33) and 0.5 M H2SO4 in water (ƞ = 1.76).

Mueller-Hinton broth (MHB) was inoculated with each bacterial species from stock plates and incubated with agitation at 120 rpm at 37 °C. After 24 h incubation, each bacterial culture was diluted to a McFarland standard of 0.5 based on optical density measurements at 600 nm. Bacterial strains included Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC 6538), Escherichia coli (ATCC 25922), Staphylococcus epidermidis (ATCC 49134), Bacillus cereus (ATCC 14579), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (ATCC 15442), Proteus mirabilis (ATCC 25933), and Streptococcus pyogenes (ATCC 19615). Each well of a sterile 96-well plate (VWR, Padnor, PA) was inoculated with 100 µL and plates were incubated in a stationary incubator at 37 °C.

To assess biofilm formation in the presence of N-doped C-dots, cultures were treated with particles at concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 1.5 mg/mL at the time of inoculation. N-doped C-dots were dispersed in sterile phosphate-buffered saline prior to treatment. After 24 h, the total, planktonic, and elution were characterized by spectrophotometer using a microplate reader (Synergy HT, BioTek). The total growth was characterized by measuring the optical density at 600 nm of each well after 24 h of incubation. The liquid media was then removed and transferred to a separate 96-well plate to quantify growth in the planktonic phase. Crystal violet staining allowed quantification of biofilm growth using methods similar to previous reports [23,28]. Briefly, the adherent biofilm in the original plate was washed with deionized water then stained with 100 µL of 0.1 % crystal violet. After 10 min, the liquid staining solution was removed, and the wells were again washed twice with 200 µL of deionized water. Elution of the crystal violet from the adherent biofilms was achieved using 200 µL absolute ethanol with agitation at 120 rpm for approximately 10 min. The eluted crystal violet was transferred to a separate 96-well plate for absorption measurements at 575 nm.

In a 96-well plate (VWR, Padnor, PA), untreated broth cultures were left to incubate for 24 h at 37 °C as detailed above to allow initial biofilm formation in the absence of C-dots. After 24 h, the planktonic growth was removed and replaced with fresh MHB. Cultures were then treated with N-doped C-dots. The plates were incubated for another 24 h at 37 °C before characterization of total, planktonic and biofilm growth as described above.

Data were analyzed using Prism 9 software (GraphPad Software Inc., San Diego, CA) by One-Way ANOVA using Dunnett’s multiple comparison post-test. p–values < 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of N-doped C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: As the C-dots were prepared via one-pot hydrothermal method using CA in the presence of PEI, that possess abundant numbers of nitrogen (N) atoms in primary, secondary, tertiary and quaternary amine forms, the obtained C-dots are N-doped. The CA molecules are carbon sources, whereas PEI molecules serve as the N sources. The schematic presentation of synthesis of N-doped C-dots is given in Fig. 1 (a). The mechanism of C-dot formation is the amide linkages that result from thermal dehydration of ammonium and carboxylate moieties by covalent attachment of excess amounts of PEI molecules with CA molecules [22]. The possible reaction mechanism is formation of a network by the reactions of the two precursors containing a large number of functional groups, followed by high temperature carbonization of the polymerized network [29]. Various functional groups e.g., hydroxyl, epoxides, carboxylic acids, and amine were reported on synthesized N-doped C-dots [30,31]. Because of the existence of various amine groups on the prepared N-doped C-dots, they can be readily treated with HCl solution to attain protonated amine groups for increased positive charge and stabilization.

The size and morphology of N-doped C-dots before and after HCl treatment were investigated by SEM. The SEM images of N-doped C-dots particles and their HCl treated forms were given in Fig. 1 (b) and (c), respectively. It is apparent that both particles tend to aggregate in small clusters. Individual particles were randomly selected, and their sizes presented in Fig. 1 (b) and (c) for N-doped C-dots before and after HCl treatment, respectively. The SEM results suggested that the sizes of N-doped C-dots varied from 50 to 100 nm approximately, whereas N-doped C-dot HCl particles presented smaller sizes ranging between 45 and 72 nm. Overall, ﻿the SEM images reveal that N-doped C-dots are mostly spherical in shape and acid treatment did not have a significant impact on the morphology of N-doped C-dots. Moreover, N-doped C-dots and N-doped C-dot-HCl were also characterized by SEM-Energy-dispersive X-ray microanalysis (EDX) to detect the presence of the atomic elements such as N, O, and Cl in the samples. SEM-EDX mapping of the N-doped C-dots and N-doped C-dot-HCl shown in Fig. 2 (a)-(f) confirms the presence of N, O atoms in both structures.

The appearance of Cl in Fig. 2 (c) with significant amount and homogeneous distribution confirms the successful conversion of N-doped C-dots to N-doped C-dot-HCl after HCl treatment. Fig. 2 (g) shows the EDX patterns of the samples. The appearance of Kα peak of Cl at 2.65 keV confirms the presence of Cl atoms in the structure [32]. Furthermore, the elemental atomic percentages of N, O and Cl of the samples in Fig. 2 (g) inset revealed the significant conversion of N-doped C-dots to N-doped C-dot-HCl.

The change on the surface charges and sizes of the N-doped C-dots are summarized in Table 1. The measured zeta potential of N-doped C-dots increased to + 23.4 ± 0.2 mV from − 2.3 ± 0.1 mV after HCl treatments, confirming the amine functionality and/or N-doping of the C-dots. The protonation of amine groups on N-doped C-dots provided highly charged surface features that contributes to their antibacterial properties.

Additionally, the sizes of the N-doped C-dot and N-doped C-dot-HCl were measured by DLS measurements to be 105 ± 19 nm and 84 ± 11 nm, respectively. The increase in the surface charge to + 23.4 ± 0.2 mV after protonation caused the homogenous dispersion of agglomerates of C-dots and the smaller size of N-doped C-dot-HCl (84 ± 11 nm) can be attributed to the reduced aggregation of protonated C-dots in comparison to their non-protonated forms.

The FT-IR spectra of N-doped C-dot and N-doped C-dot-HCl are given in Fig. 3 (a). The most distinct peaks as CO stretching peaks at 1647 cm−1, N-H bending peaks at 1551 cm−1, and C-N peaks at 1023 cm−1 were noted from FT-IR spectrum of N-doped C-dots [33]. Upon protonation, the CO stretching peaks shifted to 1717 cm−1 for N-doped C-dots, and a protonated amine peak was observed at 1649 cm−1.

The change in thermal stability of N-doped C-dots after HCl treatment was also validated via TGA measurements, and the corresponding thermograms are given in Fig. 3 (b). The N-doped C-dots and their protonated form start to degrade at similar temperatures around 130–140 °C, with continuous degradation thereafter. The thermal degradation of N-doped C-dots is completed in one step between 130 and 651 °C with 96.1 % weight loss. Further heating to 800 °C yielded 96.3 % weight loss. On the other hand, thermal degradation of N-doped C-dot-HCl started between 130 and 170 °C with 12.2 % weight loss. Then, a second degradation range was noted at 275–340 °C, with 55.6 % cumulative weight loss followed by a final step in between 350 and 530 °C with 98.3 % cumulative weight loss. It is apparent from Fig. 3 (b) that the thermal stability of N-doped C-dots and N-doped C-dot-HCl is similar (there is almost no degradation up to 130 °C), but the degradation amounts of N-doped C-dots is somewhat changed with protonation, which may eliminate a different group via reaction of the newly generated quaternary ammonium groups.

Furthermore, the change in zeta potentials of N-doped C-dots both before and after protonation was investigated by constructing a zeta potential versus pH graph. The isoelectric points (IEP) for both particles were determined, and the corresponding curves are illustrated in Fig. 4 (a). The zeta potential value of N-doped C-dots was measured as − 2.3 mV from the corresponding aqueous solutions (1 mg/mL concentration) at pH 9.1. In the acidic regions, the zeta potential of N-doped C-dots increased to positive values, e.g., at pH 2.03, the zeta potential values increased to + 22.7 ± 0.1 mV due to the protonation of the amino groups. The IEP for N-doped C-dots was determined as pH 9.04. Also, in the acidic regions, the zeta potential values of N-doped C-dot-HCl aqueous at 1 mg/mL concentration were also highly positive, e.g., the zeta potential value of N-doped C-dot-HCl particles were measured as + 24.6 ± 0.2 mV at pH 2.03. The IEP of N-doped C-dot-HCl particles was determined as pH 9.25, and the zeta potential values of these particles above this pH (IEP) were measured as negative as illustrated in Fig. 4 (a) due to the deprotonation of amine groups and the carboxylic acid groups on the N-doped C-dot particles.

The XRD patterns for N-doped C-dots and N-doped C-dot-HCl are shown in Fig. 4 (b). A broad diffraction peak at about 2θ = 20° for N-doped C-dot that is also observed for N-doped C-dot-HCl at 2θ = 25° with reduced intensity of counts is assigned to the turbostratic carbon phase at (002) crystal planes [29]. Also, the broad diffraction peaks at about 2θ = 43° for N-doped C-dots is attributed to the carbon phase at (100) crystal planes.

The fluorescent properties of both N-doped C-dots and N-doped C-dot-HCl were also investigated by recording fluorescent emission spectrum between 380 and 700 nm at 356 nm excitation wavelength via 500 kV PMT voltage and the corresponding spectra are given in Fig. 5. It was reported that the fluorescent intensity of N-doped C-dot prepared from PEI and CA precursors decreased with the increasing amount of PEI molecules to CA molecules [22].

Earlier, we reported that the fluorescent intensity of N-doped C-dots prepared from 1:1 and 1:0.5 w/w ratio of PEI and CA as 42,480 and 31,670 at 439 and 440 nm, respectively and the quantum yield percent values were also calculated as 53 ± 4.8 and 31 ± 2.7%, respectively [22]. In the present work, the observed fluorescence intensity of prepared N-doped C-dots at 3:1 w/w ratio of PEI and CA molecules was 24,540 at 439 nm. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of N-doped C-dots was found to increase after HCl treatment with the fluorescence intensity value of 41940 at 437 nm. The calculated quantum yield percent values for both N-doped C-dots and N-doped C-dot-HCl were also summarized in Table 1. The significant increase in the quantum yield percent value for N-doped C-dots from 10.5 ± 1.1 % to 52.3 ± 2.9 % upon protonation for N-doped C-dot-HCl can be attributed to the existence of a positive charge, which can enhance the excitation of molecules and increase the intensity of the emitted light.

The cytotoxicity of N-doped C-dot particles was reported earlier [22]. We noted no in vitro toxicity at a particle concentration of 3.125 mg/mL in either of the two mammalian cell lines examined, which were MCF-7 breast epithelial cells and T84 intestinal epithelial cells. Following treatment with the N-doped C-dots, viability of 100 % was observed for both cell lines.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition and Eradication of S. aureus Biofilms
ce:section.ce:para: Our previous work demonstrated that N-doped C-dots inhibited replication of selected medically relevant Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria [22]. While that study focused only on total bacterial growth after overnight incubation, the present study expands our focus to the impact of these particles on biofilm formation and eradication. We quantified the growth of S. aureus cultures treated with N-doped C-dot-HCl at the time of inoculation. After 24-hour incubation, these particles inhibited total bacterial proliferation as indicated by a statistically significant reduction in total bacterial growth at all tested concentrations as shown in Fig. 6 (a). Within each well inoculated with the bacteria, total growth is a combination of proliferation in the planktonic phase (suspended in the liquid culture media) and in the biofilm phase (adherent bacteria on the bottom and side surfaces of the well). By comparing growth in each phase after 24 h incubation, we were better able to ascertain the specific impact of N-doped C-dot-HCl treatment on S. aureus proliferation. For S. aureus, concentrations that led to reduction of total bacterial growth also induced a significant reduction in planktonic growth. This suggests a direct interference with bacterial proliferation by the N-doped C-dots, as we have previously reported with S. aureus and other bacterial species [22].

The current results further indicate that interaction between the C-dots and the replicating bacteria hinders biofilm formation. This could be a result of either the reduction in overall number of bacterial cells available for incorporation within the biofilm due to impeded replication, a direct interference with biofilm formation, or both. Representative micrographs of an untreated biofilm after 24-hour incubation and with N-doped C-dot-HCl treatment (1.5 mg/mL) at the time of incubation is provided as Supporting information (Fig. S1).

To further assess the antibiofilm properties of N-doped C-dot-HCl, untreated S. aureus cultures were incubated for 24 h to allow bacterial proliferation and biofilm formation in pure broth. After 24 h, liquid media was removed from the culture, including any bacteria growing in the planktonic phase, and wells were treated with N-doped C-dot-HCl in fresh Mueller Hinton broth at increasing concentrations for another 24 h. At this point, total, planktonic and biofilm growth was quantified. At all concentrations tested, N-doped C-dots applied to wells led to significant disruption of existing S. aureus biofilms as compared to untreated wells as illustrated in Fig. 6 (b). This suggests that the N-doped C-dot-HCl were able to directly interact with bacteria within the existing biofilm. For all treatments except the lowest concentration of 0.1 mg/mL, the amount of intact bacteria detected 24 h post-treatment of established biofilms was significantly lower than that detected at the time of treatment (at t = 24 h initial incubation with no C-dot-HCl treatment). Therefore, the extent of S. aureus within the biofilm that was formed during the initial time period was significantly reduced following treatment with the nanoparticles. There are several mechanisms that could potentially lead to the antibiofilm properties of the N-doped C-dots, including particle-bacteria interaction impeding adherence of the bacteria to the plate surface, interference with quorum sensing between bacteria, and/or disruption of the formation or integrity of the extracellular matrix (ECM) that encapsulates the bacteria within the biofilm structure [34–37]. Representative micrographs presented as Supporting information (Fig. S2) suggest that at least some of the ECM remains intact after treatment with N-doped C-dot-HCl; however, there is no intact S. aureus present to retain the crystal violet stain. This is reflective of our quantitative data presented in Fig. 6(b) and suggests that the particles interact mainly with the bacteria rather than with the components of the ECM. This will be further in explored in future studies.

We compared the efficacy of our novel nanoparticles against a chemical antibiotic, gentamicin. Gentamicin was able to significantly inhibit total bacterial proliferation at or above 0.16 µg/mL, as presented in Fig. 6(c), indicating its minimum inhibitory concentration for this bacterial strain is 0.16 µg/mL. This antibiotic significantly inhibited and biofilm formation at concentrations at or above 0.16 µg/mL and 0.032 µg/mL and above (see Fig. 6(c)). However, when assessing its ability to eradicate S. aureus from an existing biofilm, gentamicin was ineffective at concentrations up to 50 times its minimum inhibitory concentration for S. aureus as presented in Fig. 6 (d). This suggests that, unlike our N-doped C-dots, this small-molecule drug is not able to disrupt the microorganisms within an existing bacterial biofilm.

In the comparison with similar studies in the literature, it was clearly realized that the prepared N-doped C-dot-HCl particles are efficient materials with higher bacterial proliferation and biofilm formation inhibition/eradication efficiency at 1.5 mg/mL concentration in 24 h [35,38–40]. Ran et al., reported that more than 90 % biofilm of S. aureus inhibition/eradication was observed in 24 h, at a 1 mg/mL concentration of quaternized C-dots [38]. In another study, almost 90% biofilm formation of E. coli was inhibited in 24 h in the presence of biomass-based C-dots at 1 mg/mL concentration [39]. It was also reported by our group in the earlier study that, the cysteine-based C-dots at 25 mg/mL concentration inhibited biofilm formation of S. aureus [35]. Furthermore, Li et al., also stated that very fast inhibition of biofilm formation of S. aureus in 60 min in the presence of 250 µg/mL cationic C-dots [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-doped C-dot-HCl biofilm inhibition with other relevant organisms
ce:section.ce:para: In assessing the breadth of the antibiofilm properties of N-doped C-dots, we quantified biofilm formation of other medically relevant Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. These included Gram-positive B. cereus, S. epidermidis, and S. pyogenes and Gram-negative E. coli, P. mirabilis and P. aeruginosa. Total growth was significantly inhibited for all organisms treated with the C-dots, as indicated in Fig. 7. Interestingly, only for B. cereus, S. epidermidis, and P. aeruginosa was this reduction in total growth reflected in decreased planktonic growth. However, biofilm formation by all organisms was reduced significantly upon treatment with the nanoparticles, including those for which total and/or planktonic growth was not impacted. Lin et al. also noted inhibition of biofilm formation by E. coli with no impact on bacterial proliferation upon treatment with their biocompatible carbon dots [39]. The impact of specific nanoparticle formulations on bacterial replication and/or biofilm formation is an important distinction and further emphasizes the antibacterial and antibiofilm properties of N-doped C-dot-HCl.

Work by Wang et. al., suggests that C-dots may interfere with membrane integrity of the bacteria [23]. This potential mechanism of action could explain the dose-dependent inhibition of the planktonic phase, and the decrease in rate of biofilm formation by S. aureus, B. cereus, S. epidermidis, and P. aeruginosa. In a recent study, Otis et. al., showed the occurrence of morphological changes when P. aeruginosa was treated with aminoguanidine-based C-dots, such as roughening of the cell surface and compression of the cells [41]. The direct interactions between the nanoparticles and the bacterial membrane resulted in inhibited bacterial proliferation and biofilm formation. These particles were selective for P. aeruginosa as there were no effects noted with S. aureus.

The N-doped C-dot-HCl’s positive zeta potential at physiological pH as illustrated in Fig. 4 (a), favors interaction with the anionic bacterial membrane. Recent studies show direct association of positively-charged carbon dots with S. aureus [38] , and S. epidermidis [42] as indicated by an increase in zeta potential of the organisms upon treatment with the positively-charged particles. In addition to their direct interaction with microbial cells, the size and surface chemistry of nanoparticles play an integral role in their ability to successfully penetrate the ECM of biofilms. Given the complex nature of ECMs, which tend to be rich in environmental DNA, polysaccharides, and proteins, the penetration of C-dots likely relies on electrostatic interactions with molecules both on the surface and embedded within the biofilm [24]. Possible inhibitory mechanisms include bacterial membrane disruption, as mentioned previously, generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) as has been previously postulated [23,40], and the nanoparticles acting as decoy complexes that compete with other structural components within the ECM, thereby inhibiting biofilm maturation and/or the structural integrity of existing biofilms [43]. The specific mechanism(s) by which N-doped C-dot-HCl exert an inhibitory effect on biofilms and a destructive effect on the mature biofilm integrity will be explored in future studies. Such insight will allow us to tailor N-doped C-dot formulations to optimize their antibiofilm properties for clinical and industrial applications.

Biofilms presents challenges in both the medical and industrial arenas, causing a great deal of health-related problems as well as food, air conditioning and water related challenges with enormous economic burden. Therefore, further exploration into effective preventive and therapeutic strategies whether alone or in combination with other antimicrobials is needed. Here, we successfully identified N-doped carbon-dots as a potential anti-biofilm agent. The use of these C-dots was effective in inhibiting bacterial proliferation in the planktonic phase, subsequently preventing biofilm formation. Furthermore, these N-doped C-dot-HCl were also effective in dispersing bacteria from established biofilms. As compared to the commonly used antibiotics e.g., gentamicin, we showed that the C-dots were able to significantly eradicate bacteria from mature biofilms, whereas gentamicin was not even at concentrations 50 times its MIC. While the planktonic phase of bacterial growth was not affected by the PEI-CA C-dots for all microbes tested, biofilm development was still significantly reduced. These findings provide insights into the use of these biocompatible N-doped C-dots and their potential application for both clinical and industrial use. Other applications, including incorporation of N-doped carbon-dots into a surface coating for biomedical implants, catheters, contact lenses and even sutures, are under investigation.

Wakeem L. Abraham: Data curation; Formal analysis; Investigation; Methodology; Writing - original draft, Sahin Demirci: Data curation; Formal analysis; Investigation; Methodology; Writing - original draft, Madison S. Wypyski: Data curation; Formal analysis; Investigation; Methodology; Writing - original draft,Ramesh S. Ayyala: Funding acquisition; Resources, Venkat R. Brethanabotla: Funding acquisition; Resources; Writing - review & editing, Louise Lawson: Formal analysis; Funding acquisition; Investigation; Methodology; Resources; Supervision; Visualization; Writing - review & editing, Nurettin Sahiner: Conceptualization; Formal analysis; Funding acquisition; Investigation; Methodology; Project administration; Resources; Supervision; Visualization; Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing interest in relation to the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the ASPiRE Program at Tulane University School of Medicine and the Tulane University Honors Summer Research Program. This work was also partially funded by the University of South Florida start up fund to NS.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2022.112704.
